---
title: "Quiz 3: Confounding & ANCOVA"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## About this quiz
**Module:** Confounding & ANCOVA  ·  **Available after:** Week 4  ·  **Source:** Lecture Notes, [Ch. 2 (ANCOVA)](../../notes/chapters/02-ancova.html).

This quiz assesses analysis of covariance and confounding as drilled in Homework 3: the parallel-lines model in which two groups share one covariate slope and differ only in intercept, the unadjusted group difference from a dummy-only model versus the adjusted difference from a model that controls for a covariate, the meaning of "controlling for" a covariate, computing adjusted group means at a fixed covariate value, what makes a variable a confounder, how adjustment can shrink or even reverse a raw group difference, and why collinearity inflates standard errors without biasing the coefficients. Items are open-book and emphasize interpretation over computation.
:::

## Blackboard settings

| Setting | Value |
|:--------|:------|
| Number of questions | 10 |
| Total points | 13 |
| Time limit | 60 minutes |
| Attempts | Unlimited |
| Score recorded | Highest |
| Availability | After Week 4 |
| Show correct answers | After the availability window closes |

: Deployment settings for Quiz 3. Time limit and answer-release timing are proposed defaults (see the [Blackboard Setup](../appendices/blackboard-setup.qmd) appendix). {.striped}

## Questions

### Question 1 — Parallel lines in the ANCOVA model {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Multiple Choice · 1 pt · Conceptual · Notes Ch. 2]`**

A researcher models years of education ($y$) from father's education ($x$, continuous) and a white/non-white dummy ($z$, coded $z = 1$ for white and $z = 0$ for non-white) with the additive model

$$\hat{y} = 3 + 0.8x - 0.6z.$$

What is the geometric relationship between the fitted line for whites and the fitted line for non-whites?

A. The two lines have the same slope ($0.8$) and differ only in their intercepts, so they are parallel.

B. One line has slope $0.8$ and the other has slope $-0.6$, so the lines cross.

C. The two lines have different slopes but the same intercept, so they fan out from a common point.

D. The two lines are identical, because a single dummy cannot change the fitted equation.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: A**

- **Why A is correct:** With no interaction term, the dummy $z$ enters only additively, so it shifts the intercept without touching the slope. Non-whites ($z = 0$) follow $\hat{y} = 3 + 0.8x$ and whites ($z = 1$) follow $\hat{y} = 2.4 + 0.8x$; both lines have the **same slope** ($0.8$), separated by a constant vertical gap. Same slope, different intercept means the lines are parallel.
- **Why not B:** This is the HW3 Problem 2(b) misconception that "one line has slope $\beta_1$ and the other has slope $\beta_2$." The coefficient $-0.6$ multiplies the *dummy*, not $x$, so it is an intercept shift, not a second slope; a different slope per group would require an $x \times z$ interaction term, which is absent here.
- **Why not C:** It is the *intercept* that differs by group, not the slope. Differing slopes that share an intercept describe an interaction (non-parallel lines crossing at $x = 0$), not the additive ANCOVA model, which forces one common slope.
- **Why not D:** The dummy *does* change the equation — it lowers the white line's intercept by $0.6$; the two lines would coincide only if the dummy coefficient were zero.
- **Source:** [Ch. 2 (ANCOVA), "The ANCOVA Model"](../../notes/chapters/02-ancova.html#sec-ch2-model).

:::

### Question 2 — Reading an unadjusted group difference {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Fill-in-the-Blank · 2 pts · Applied · Notes Ch. 2]`**

A dummy-only regression of years of education ($y$) on a white/non-white dummy ($z$, coded $z = 1$ for white and $z = 0$ for non-white), with no covariate, gives

$$\hat{y} = 11 + 2z.$$

What is the **unadjusted difference** in predicted years of education, **white minus non-white**? Enter a number (you may include a sign).

Unadjusted difference (white − non-white) = ________

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: 2** (accept **1.9–2.1**, tolerance $\pm 0.1$)

- **Why 2:** The white mean is $11 + 2(1) = 13$ and the non-white mean is $11 + 2(0) = 11$, so white − non-white $= 13 - 11 = +2$ years. In a dummy-only model the dummy coefficient *is* the raw difference in group means, so the answer reads directly off the coefficient on $z$ — whites are estimated to have 2 more years of education than non-whites, before any adjustment.
- **Tolerance:** the $\pm 0.1$ window absorbs trivial rounding without admitting a wrong method.
- **Common errors:** answering `13` or `11` reports a single *group mean* rather than the difference; answering `-2` flips the sign (it is white minus non-white, not non-white minus white) — all fall outside the window.
- **Source:** [Ch. 2 (ANCOVA), "Comparing Unadjusted vs Adjusted Differences"](../../notes/chapters/02-ancova.html#comparing-unadjusted-vs-adjusted-differences).

:::

### Question 3 — The adjusted difference as a constant gap {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Multiple Choice · 1 pt · Conceptual · Notes Ch. 2]`**

In the adjusted model $\hat{y} = 3 + 0.8x - 0.6z$ (with $y$ = years of education, $x$ = father's education, $z = 1$ white / $z = 0$ non-white), the coefficient on $z$ is $-0.6$. Which statement correctly describes this $-0.6$?

A. The constant vertical gap between the two parallel lines — the adjusted white − non-white difference — the same at every value of father's education.

B. The gap between the two lines only at $x = 0$; at other values of father's education the gap differs.

C. The raw difference of $+2$, just written with the opposite sign.

D. A gap that grows wider as father's education increases.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: A**

- **Why A is correct:** At any fixed $x$, the white − non-white difference is $(2.4 + 0.8x) - (3 + 0.8x) = -0.6$ — the $x$ terms cancel, so the gap is **constant** at every value of father's education. Because the lines are parallel, $\beta_2 = -0.6$ is the adjusted group difference and equals the constant vertical distance between them: whites are predicted to have $0.6$ *fewer* years of education than non-whites at the same father's education.
- **Why not B:** The constant gap is a property of the parallel-lines model at *every* $x$, not only at $x = 0$; the intercept shift does not "wear off" away from zero.
- **Why not C:** $-0.6$ is the *adjusted* difference and is genuinely negative; it is not the unadjusted $+2$ re-signed. Adjustment changes the magnitude *and* the sign — it is a different quantity, not a relabeling.
- **Why not D:** A gap that grows with $x$ would require non-parallel lines, i.e., an $x \times z$ interaction; in the additive model the gap never changes with father's education.
- **Source:** [Ch. 2 (ANCOVA), "Comparing Unadjusted vs Adjusted Differences"](../../notes/chapters/02-ancova.html#comparing-unadjusted-vs-adjusted-differences).

:::

### Question 4 — The meaning of "controlling for" {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Multiple Choice · 1 pt · Conceptual · Notes Ch. 2]`**

In the fitted model $\hat{y} = 3 + 0.8x - 0.6z$, the $-0.6$ is the white − non-white difference "controlling for father's education." What does "controlling for father's education" actually mean here?

A. Whites and non-whites were physically recruited so that the two groups had identical father's-education distributions before the data were collected.

B. Father's education was removed from the dataset so that it can no longer influence years of education.

C. The group difference is computed by comparing a white and a non-white person *at the same value of father's education*; it is a statistical operation within the regression, not a physical manipulation.

D. Because father's education is held constant, the $-0.6$ automatically has a causal interpretation.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: C**

- **Why C is correct:** "Holding father's education constant" means the model compares a white and a non-white person who share the same value of $x$; the predicted gap between them is exactly $-0.6$ regardless of which common $x$ is chosen. This is a *statistical* adjustment carried out inside the regression, not a physical intervention on anyone.
- **Why not A:** No one was recruited to balance father's education; the groups differ on it in the data. Controlling for $x$ statistically adjusts for that imbalance — it does not pretend the imbalance was removed by design.
- **Why not B:** Father's education is not discarded; it remains in the model as a predictor. "Controlling for" a variable means *including* it, not deleting it.
- **Why not D:** A causal reading requires that *all* relevant confounders be accounted for. Statistically controlling for one covariate does not, by itself, license a causal claim about race.
- **Source:** [Ch. 2 (ANCOVA), "Estimation and Interpretation"](../../notes/chapters/02-ancova.html#sec-ch2-estimation).

:::

### Question 5 — Computing adjusted group means {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Fill-in-Multiple-Blanks · 2 pts · Applied · Notes Ch. 2]`**

Using the adjusted model $\hat{y} = 3 + 0.8x - 0.6z$ (with $y$ = years of education, $x$ = father's education, $z = 1$ white / $z = 0$ non-white), evaluate the predicted means at **father's education $x = 11$** and complete the sentence: "At $x = 11$, the predicted education is **[blank1]** years for non-whites and **[blank2]** years for whites, so the white − non-white adjusted difference is **[blank3]** years."

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answers:** `[blank1]` = 11.8 (accept 11.7–11.9, tolerance $\pm 0.1$) · `[blank2]` = 11.2 (accept 11.1–11.3, tolerance $\pm 0.1$) · `[blank3]` = −0.6 (accept −0.7 to −0.5, tolerance $\pm 0.1$)

- **`[blank1]` (non-white, $z = 0$):** $\hat{y} = 3 + 0.8(11) - 0.6(0) = 3 + 8.8 = 11.8$ years.
- **`[blank2]` (white, $z = 1$):** $\hat{y} = 3 + 0.8(11) - 0.6(1) = 11.8 - 0.6 = 11.2$ years.
- **`[blank3]` (white − non-white):** $11.2 - 11.8 = -0.6$ years — the same constant adjusted gap given by the dummy coefficient, here whites $0.6$ years lower.
- **Common errors:** dropping the $-0.6$ dummy term reports $11.8$ for *both* groups (gap $0$); omitting the $0.8x$ slope term returns the bare intercepts ($3$ and $2.4$); reporting $+0.6$ flips the sign of the gap. The $\pm 0.1$ windows exclude each of these.
- **Source:** [Ch. 2 (ANCOVA), "Adjusted Group Means"](../../notes/chapters/02-ancova.html#adjusted-group-means).

:::

### Question 6 — What makes a variable a confounder {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Multiple Choice · 1 pt · Conceptual · Notes Ch. 2]`**

A researcher compares years of education across racial groups and worries that **father's education** confounds the comparison. For father's education to be a confounder of the race–education relationship, what must be true?

A. Father's education must be associated with the outcome (years of education) only.

B. Father's education must be associated with the predictor (race) only.

C. Father's education must be associated with **both** the predictor (race) and the outcome (years of education).

D. Any third variable measured alongside race and education automatically qualifies as a confounder.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: C**

- **Why C is correct:** A confounder is a variable associated with **both** the predictor and the outcome. Father's education qualifies because it is related to race (the groups differ in average father's education) *and* to years of education (children of more-educated fathers tend to get more education). That dual association is exactly why ignoring it distorts the raw race comparison.
- **Why not A:** A variable related to the outcome *only* — independent of race — does not bias the race coefficient; it cannot create a spurious race–education association.
- **Why not B:** A variable related to the predictor *only* — independent of the outcome — likewise cannot confound, since it carries no information about education.
- **Why not D:** Being a "third variable" is not the criterion. A variable must be associated with both the predictor and the outcome; many third variables (e.g., ones unrelated to either) are not confounders.
- **Source:** [Ch. 2 (ANCOVA), "Confounding and Endogeneity"](../../notes/chapters/02-ancova.html#sec-ch2-confounding).

:::

### Question 7 — Adjustment changes the estimand {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[True/False · 1 pt · Conceptual · Notes Ch. 2]`**

**True or False:** Because the white/non-white dummy coefficient means the same thing whether or not father's education is in the model, the $+2$ from the dummy-only model and the $-0.6$ from the adjusted model are just two roundings of a single number.

A. True

B. False

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: B (False)**

- **Why B is correct:** Adding father's education *changes the estimand*. The dummy-only coefficient is the **unadjusted** difference ($+2$); the coefficient with father's education included is the **adjusted** difference at a common value of father's education ($-0.6$). Here adjustment does not merely shrink the gap — it **reverses its sign**, from $+2$ to $-0.6$. The two coefficients answer different questions; they are not two roundings of one number.
- **Why not A (True):** Treating the dummy coefficient as fixed "with or without $x$" is the documented misconception that "controlling for $x$" is decoration. The words "controlling for father's education" change *which comparison* the coefficient describes — a raw group difference versus a within-father's-education difference.
- **Source:** [Ch. 2 (ANCOVA), "Comparing Unadjusted vs Adjusted Differences"](../../notes/chapters/02-ancova.html#comparing-unadjusted-vs-adjusted-differences).

:::

### Question 8 — Predicting the direction of the adjusted gap {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Multiple Choice · 1 pt · Challenge · Notes Ch. 2]`**

In the United States, mean annual income for Black people ($\mu_1$) is lower than for white people ($\mu_2$). Education is lower on average for Black people, and income is positively related to education. Compared with the raw gap $\mu_2 - \mu_1$, the white − Black income difference **controlling for education** is:

A. Greater than $\mu_2 - \mu_1$.

B. Less than $\mu_2 - \mu_1$.

C. Possibly equal to $\mu_2 - \mu_1$.

D. Unknowable without fitting the model.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: B**

- **Why B is correct:** Education is a confounder here — lower on average for Black people and positively related to income — so part of the raw income gap reflects the education gap. Comparing the two groups *at the same education level* removes that education-driven portion, so the education-adjusted income difference is **less than** the raw $\mu_2 - \mu_1$.
- **Why not A:** Adjustment widens the gap only when the covariate suppresses the raw difference. Here education is higher in the group with higher raw income and positively tied to income, so adjusting *removes* a positive contribution — it cannot enlarge the gap.
- **Why not C:** Because education is associated with both group and income, controlling for it **will**, given these associations, change the coefficient — it is left exactly unchanged only in the degenerate (knife-edge) case where the relevant associations cancel.
- **Why not D:** The qualitative direction *is* knowable from the confounding setup — a covariate higher in the higher-income group and positively related to income shrinks the gap. The sign of the change is determined by the stated associations, without estimating the model.
- **Source:** [Ch. 2 (ANCOVA), "Omitted Variable Bias"](../../notes/chapters/02-ancova.html#sec-ch2-ovb).

:::

### Question 9 — A confounder must change the coefficient {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[True/False · 1 pt · Conceptual · Notes Ch. 2]`**

**True or False:** If a covariate is associated with **both** the predictor and the outcome, then adding it to the model can still leave the predictor's coefficient *exactly* unchanged.

A. True

B. False

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answer: B (False)**

- **Why B is correct:** By the omitted-variable-bias logic, the change in a coefficient when a covariate is added is governed by the covariate's association with *both* the predictor and the outcome. If the covariate is related to both (a confounder), that product is nonzero, so adding it **will**, given these associations, change the coefficient — it is left exactly unchanged only in the degenerate (knife-edge) case where the relevant associations cancel. The change may be small, but it is generically not exactly zero.
- **Why not A (True):** Expecting a both-related covariate to leave the coefficient "exactly" unchanged contradicts the OVB formula; that outcome is precisely what the confounding setup rules out. A coefficient is left exactly unchanged only when the covariate is unrelated to the predictor *or* to the outcome — in which case it is not a confounder of that relationship.
- **Source:** [Ch. 2 (ANCOVA), "The OVB Formula"](../../notes/chapters/02-ancova.html#the-ovb-formula).

:::

### Question 10 — Collinearity: precision, not bias {.unnumbered}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Question

**`[Multiple Answer · 2 pts · Challenge · Notes Ch. 2]`**

Two predictors in a regression — father's education and a closely related covariate — are highly collinear (strongly correlated with each other). **Select all** statements that are true.

A. Collinearity inflates the standard errors of the affected coefficients, widening their confidence intervals.

B. Collinearity leaves the coefficient estimates unbiased — in expectation they remain centered on their true values.

C. Bias instead comes from *omitting* a relevant confounder, not from collinearity among included predictors.

D. Collinearity biases the coefficient estimates toward zero, making the effects systematically too small.

E. A large standard error is itself evidence that a coefficient is biased.

#### Answer & Rationale

**Correct answers: A, B, C**

- **Why A is correct:** Collinearity makes the predictors' unique contributions hard to separate, which inflates the standard errors (the variance inflation factor measures exactly this) and widens the confidence intervals.
- **Why B is correct:** Collinearity is a *variance* (precision) problem, not a *bias* problem — the point estimates stay centered on the true coefficients even as the intervals blow up.
- **Why C is correct:** Systematic bias arises from *omitting* a relevant confounder (omitted-variable bias), not from correlation among the predictors already in the model. Including a collinear predictor costs precision, not unbiasedness.
- **Why not D:** This conflates imprecision with bias. Collinearity does not pull the estimates toward zero; their expectation is unchanged.
- **Why not E:** A large standard error signals *low precision*, not bias. Unbiased estimates can have large standard errors, and biased estimates can have small ones — the size of the SE says nothing about bias.
- **Source:** [Ch. 2 (ANCOVA), "The Effect of Collinearity"](../../notes/chapters/02-ancova.html#the-effect-of-collinearity).

:::

## Coverage

This quiz draws entirely from Lecture Notes Ch. 2 (ANCOVA), mirroring Homework 3's race + father's-education equations (the raw difference $+2$ reverses to the adjusted difference $-0.6$) and folding in the collinearity-vs-bias diagnostic. The 10 items total **13 points** (Multiple Choice ×5, True/False ×2, Fill-in-the-Blank ×1, Fill-in-Multiple-Blanks ×1, Multiple Answer ×1) and span all three difficulty levels, rising from reading the parallel-lines model to predicting the direction of adjustment and the collinearity-versus-bias distinction.

| # | Concept | Type | Difficulty | Pts |
|:--|:--------|:-----|:-----------|:---:|
| 1 | Parallel lines: shared slope, different intercept | Multiple Choice | Conceptual | 1 |
| 2 | Read the unadjusted difference from a dummy-only model (+2) | Fill-in-the-Blank | Applied | 2 |
| 3 | Adjusted difference = constant vertical gap (−0.6) | Multiple Choice | Conceptual | 1 |
| 4 | Meaning of "controlling for" a covariate | Multiple Choice | Conceptual | 1 |
| 5 | Compute adjusted group means at a fixed $x$ (11.8, 11.2, −0.6) | Fill-in-Multiple-Blanks | Applied | 2 |
| 6 | A confounder is associated with both predictor and outcome | Multiple Choice | Conceptual | 1 |
| 7 | Adjustment changes the estimand; can reverse the sign | True/False | Conceptual | 1 |
| 8 | Predict the direction of the adjusted-vs-raw gap | Multiple Choice | Challenge | 1 |
| 9 | A both-related covariate must change the coefficient | True/False | Conceptual | 1 |
| 10 | Collinearity inflates SE but does not bias coefficients | Multiple Answer | Challenge | 2 |

: Quiz 3 item map. {.striped}
